notes.anchor: you most carefully warned the Senate
notes.note: As consul in 51 BC, Servius Sulpicius Rufus had urged the Senate to remember the horrors of past civil wars and to avoid forcing a confrontation with Caesar. By the time of this letter (about 46 BC, after Caesar's victory) Cicero presents that warning as tragically vindicated.

notes.anchor: as if from some watchtower, I saw the coming storm
notes.note: A standard ancient image (the philosopher or statesman watching the tempest from a height) for political foresight; Cicero claims he and Servius both foresaw the civil war that ordinary politicians ignored.

notes.anchor: as it were among the ruined walls of the state
notes.note: Latin parietinae means the crumbling walls of a gutted building. Cicero casts the Republic after Caesar's victory as a ruined house, its institutions standing only as a shell.

notes.anchor: Caesar himself judges you as he does
notes.note: Written when Caesar, victorious in the civil war, was effectively master of Rome. Cicero reassures Servius that even the dictator esteems his integrity, a delicate compliment given that Servius had supported the losing Pompeian side and now held a post under Caesar.

notes.anchor: have turned all my care and labor to philosophy
notes.note: With public life closed off under Caesar's autocracy, Cicero withdrew to write the great philosophical works of 46--44 BC. Here he commends the same consolation to Servius, who as a famous jurist was a fellow man of learning.

notes.anchor: Your son Servius, in all the liberal arts
notes.note: The younger Servius Sulpicius Rufus, the addressee's son, was then in Rome and a frequent companion of Cicero. The "liberal arts" (artes ingenuae) are the studies fit for a free-born gentleman: literature, rhetoric, and philosophy.
